label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, so I have this problem here about an 8×8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed. The question is whether the remaining 62 squares can be perfectly tiled with 31 dominoes, each covering two adjacent squares. Hmm, interesting. Let me try to figure this out step by step. First, I know that a standard domino covers exactly two squares. Since the chessboard originally has 64 squares, removing two leaves 62 squares, which would require 31 dominoes to cover if it's possible. So the first thing that comes to mind is the classic problem where you have a chessboard with two squares removed and trying to tile it with dominoes. I remember there are some parity or coloring arguments involved here. Let me recall: a standard chessboard has alternating black and white squares, right? Each domino, when placed on the board, will always cover one black and one white square, no matter how it's placed—horizontally or vertically. That’s because adjacent squares are always opposite colors. So if the entire board is covered by dominoes, the number of black and white squares must be equal. Each domino accounts for one of each, so if the total number of squares is even, which it is here (62), then the number of black and white squares should each be 31. But wait, if we remove two diagonally opposite corners... Let me visualize a chessboard. The corners are all the same color, right? Because on a chessboard, the corners are all alternating. For example, the bottom-left corner is black, then the next square is white, and so on. Since the board is 8x8, which is even, the top-right corner will be the same color as the bottom-left corner. Similarly, the bottom-right and top-left corners are the opposite color. So if we remove two diagonally opposite corners, say, the bottom-left and top-right, both of which are black, then we've removed two black squares. Originally, the chessboard has 32 black and 32 white squares. Removing two black squares leaves us with 30 black squares and 32 white squares. That’s a difference of two squares. But each domino must cover one black and one white. Therefore, if there's an imbalance in the number of black and white squares, it's impossible to tile the board completely with dominoes. Because each domino would still require a 1:1 ratio of black and white squares. So in this case, with 30 black and 32 white squares, there's no way to pair them all up. Therefore, the tiling should be impossible. Hence, the answer should be no. But wait, let me check again to make sure I didn't make a mistake. Let me confirm the color of the diagonally opposite corners. Suppose the bottom-left corner is a black square. Then moving right, the squares alternate black and white. Since there are 8 squares in a row, the bottom row will end with a black square at position (1,8) because 8 is even, so starting from black, even positions are black? Wait, no. Wait, if the first square (1,1) is black, then (1,2) is white, (1,3) black, ..., (1,8) would be black if 8 is even? Wait, 8 is even. Wait, starting at 1 (black), then even columns would be white? Wait, no. Wait, position (row, column). So if row 1, column 1 is black, then column 1 is black, column 2 is white, column 3 black, ..., column 8 (which is even) would be white? Wait, 8 columns. If column 1 is black, column 2 white, column 3 black, column 4 white, column 5 black, column 6 white, column 7 black, column 8 white. So the bottom-left corner (1,1) is black, and the bottom-right corner (1,8) is white. Similarly, the top-left corner (8,1) would be... Let's see, since there are 8 rows. Each row starts with the opposite color of the row before. So row 1 starts with black, row 2 starts with white, row 3 black, etc. So row 8 (even) starts with white. Therefore, the top-left corner (8,1) is white, and the top-right corner (8,8) would be... column 8 is white, but since row 8 starts with white, column 8 would be... for row 8, column 1 is white, column 2 black, ..., column 8 is white (since 8 is even: starting with white, column 1: white, column 2: black, column 3: white, ..., column 8: white). Therefore, the top-right corner (8,8) is white. Wait, so the bottom-left corner (1,1) is black, and top-right corner (8,8) is white. Wait, so they are different colors? But the problem says "two diagonally opposite corner squares removed". So there are two pairs of diagonally opposite corners: (1,1) and (8,8); and (1,8) and (8,1). So (1,1) is black and (8,8) is white. Whereas (1,8) would be white (as we saw earlier) and (8,1) is white (as row 8, column 1 is white). Wait, no, (8,1) is white, but (1,8) is white as well? Wait, let me check again. Row 1: columns alternate starting with black. So column 1: black, column 2: white, ..., column 8: white. Therefore, (1,8) is white. Row 8: starts with white (since even row), so column 1: white, column 2: black, ..., column 8: white. Therefore, (8,1) is white and (8,8) is white. Therefore, the two diagonally opposite corners (1,1) and (8,8) are black and white. Whereas the other pair, (1,8) and (8,1), are both white. Wait, so if you remove two diagonally opposite corners, depending on which pair you remove, you might be removing two squares of the same color or different colors? Wait, but in an 8x8 chessboard, the two pairs of diagonal corners: one pair will be one black and one white, and the other pair will be both the same color? Wait, let me think again. The chessboard has alternating colors. For any rectangle, the opposite corners are the same color if the number of rows and columns are both even? Wait, no. Wait, in a rectangle with even number of squares along both dimensions, moving from one corner to the opposite, you have an odd number of squares? Wait, for example, in an 8x8 board, moving from (1,1) to (8,8), how many squares is that? 8 rows and 8 columns. But the number of squares along the diagonal is 8, but the color alternates each square. Starting from black, after even number of squares, you end up on the same color? Wait, if you go from (1,1) which is black, then moving to (1,2) is white, then (1,3) black... to get to (8,8), how many steps? Well, if you move from (1,1) to (8,8), you move 7 rows down and 7 columns to the right. Each move of one square changes the color. So total number of moves is 14, which is even. Therefore, starting from black, after even number of moves, you end on black. Therefore, (8,8) is same color as (1,1). Wait, but earlier when I tried to check, I thought (8,8) was white. Maybe I made a mistake there. Wait, let's clarify. Let me define the color of square (row, column) as black if row + column is even, and white if row + column is odd. Let's take (1,1): 1+1=2, even, so black. (1,8): 1+8=9, odd, white. (8,1): 8+1=9, odd, white. (8,8): 8+8=16, even, black. So indeed, (1,1) and (8,8) are both black; (1,8) and (8,1) are both white. So removing two diagonally opposite corners would mean removing either two black squares or two white squares. Therefore, in either case, you are removing two squares of the same color. Therefore, the original board had 32 black and 32 white squares. After removal, we have 30 black and 32 white, or 32 black and 30 white, depending on which corners are removed. Either way, there's an imbalance of two squares. Since each domino must cover one black and one white, you need equal numbers. Therefore, it's impossible. But let me make sure that domino tiling requires exactly that. Suppose there is a tiling. Then the number of black squares equals the number of white squares. Since after removal, we don't have that, there can't be a tiling. Therefore, the answer is no. But wait, maybe there's a different way? Maybe some dominoes can be placed such that... no, dominoes always cover one black and one white square regardless of placement. Whether you place them horizontally or vertically, they are always on adjacent squares, which are different colors. Therefore, the total number of dominoes would have to satisfy that the total number of black and white squares are equal. Since they aren't, it's impossible. Therefore, the answer is no. I think that's solid. The classic chessboard domino tiling problem
